title: Lamb fricassee
subtitle: Greek braised lamb with lettuce and egg-lemon sauce
excerpt: Lamb pieces braised with spring onions, dill, and cos lettuce until tender, then finished with a thick, warming egg-lemon sauce (avgolemono) poured over the top. A dish that is unexpected and deeply Greek: creamy from the eggs, sharp from the lemon, fragrant from the dill.
sourceNotes: Greek fricassee (φρικασέ αρνί) is documented in Greek household cooking from at least the nineteenth century, and differs substantially from the French fricassée. The Greek version uses avgolemono (egg-lemon sauce) as the finishing sauce, a technique common across Greek and Sephardic Jewish cooking in the eastern Mediterranean. The lamb-lettuce-dill combination is particularly associated with spring cooking.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Braise the lamb to the point just before adding the avgolemono; make and add the sauce to order on reheating.

content.text: The avgolemono is added off the heat at the very end: whisked egg yolks thinned with lemon juice, then tempered with hot broth before being poured back over the lamb. It thickens from the egg proteins and the lemon holds the whole sauce in a savoury balance between richness and sharpness. Do not let it boil after the eggs go in or the sauce will scramble.

Cos lettuce (romaine) wilts into the braising liquid over the last fifteen minutes, contributing a gentle vegetal note and absorbing the lamb's fat. It seems counterintuitive to cook lettuce for fifteen minutes, but it holds its structure better than you expect and is entirely correct here.

items.prepNote: outer leaves removed, cut into wedges

content.text: Heat the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} in a casserole over medium-high heat. Brown the {{lamb-shoulder}} pieces in batches, 3 minutes per side. Remove and set aside. Add the {{spring-onion}} and cook for 2 minutes. Return the lamb. Pour in the {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock and half the {{dill}}. Season well. Cover and simmer gently over low heat for 50 minutes until the lamb is tender.

Tuck the {{cos-lettuce}} wedges among the lamb pieces. Cover and cook for a further 15 minutes until the lettuce is wilted and tender. Remove from the heat. Ladle about 150 ml of the hot braising liquid into a jug.

Whisk the {{egg-yolks}} with the {{lemon}} juice until combined. Slowly whisk in the hot braising liquid in a steady stream, then pour this tempered mixture back over the lamb in the casserole. Swirl to distribute — do not stir vigorously or the sauce will become stringy. The sauce will thicken gently from the residual heat. Scatter the remaining {{dill}} over and serve immediately.

items.cause: Added to a boiling pan, or tempered too quickly
items.fix: Remove the pan from heat completely; let it cool for 1 minute; whisk the hot liquid into the eggs very slowly before returning to the pot

items.symptom: Sauce is too thin and watery
items.cause: Not enough egg yolk for the volume of liquid
items.fix: 3 yolks is the correct amount for 300 ml of broth; don't reduce this

items.cause: Cooked too long after adding the lettuce
items.fix: 15 minutes is sufficient; cos lettuce holds better than softer varieties — but don't exceed 20 minutes

items.symptom: Sauce is too lemony and sharp
items.cause: Too much lemon juice for the quantity of egg
items.fix: Taste the lemon-egg mixture before adding to the braise; adjust with a little warm water if too sharp

content.text: The avgolemono technique (using whisked egg yolks and lemon juice to enrich and thicken a hot liquid) is one of the defining techniques of Greek cooking, appearing in soups, braises, and sauces across the cuisine. It is related to similar egg-lemon emulsions in Sephardic Jewish cooking across the eastern Mediterranean, where the technique was widespread before the Greek culinary tradition codified it. In Greek fricassee, the technique is applied to a braised meat: the eggs provide richness, the lemon provides sharpness, and the combination produces a sauce that belongs to neither the cream tradition of France nor the tomato tradition of southern Italian cooking — it is distinctly Greek.
